data.title: SA Asks: How will changes at FDA impact vaccine makers?
data.description:
Vaccine stocks have been under significant pressure in recent days following news that the FDA director who oversaw the agency's vaccine program has resigned over concerns that newly appointed HHS officials were promoting misinformation about immunizations. 

Seeking Alpha analysts Stephen Ayers and Terry Chrisomalis weigh in on how management changes at the agency could impact vaccine makers.

Stephen Ayers: Dr. Peter Marks' (a top vaccine regulator) resignation signals that the Trump administration means business - for better or worse. It creates uncertainty and the market hates that! Notably, Moderna’s (NASDAQ:MRNA) 9% haircut on Monday spoke volumes. The company has two FDA-approved vaccines and is awaiting word on a few more. 

If regulators increase scrutiny of vaccines, as HHS secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has alluded to in the past, this could have major repercussions for vaccine developers. Companies like Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA) spend years playing by the rules of the FDA. If authorities change the rules mid-game, it’ll be challenging for drug developers to come out on top. 

Specifically, data requirements may tighten. This could involve longer safety follow-ups or more people tested prior to approval. Either way, you’re extending the development timeline. Regulatory applications could face intense scrutiny and be rejected for even minor concerns, pushing back commercialization. 

It also creates a “snowball effect,” particularly for mRNA-focused companies like Moderna (MRNA), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) and BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX). The public is already wary of mRNA technology. Increased scrutiny from authorities on the issue could exacerbate negative public perceptions and influence healthcare decisions. 

All in all, these developments may impact the entire lifecycle of vaccines (clinical development, regulatory approval and market adoption).

Terry Chrisomalis: In my opinion, I don’t think there will be a long-term impact on stocks such as Moderna (MRNA), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) and BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX), just to name a few. The reason why is that there have been no sweeping statements made as of yet as to what changes will be made. What might cause an issue for companies like Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ), Merck (NYSE:MRK), Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY), AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) and GlaxoSmithKline (NYSE:GSK) would be tariffs potentially being levied on vaccines, which hasn’t been done before.

data.title: CenterPoint Energy started Buy at Jefferies: 'Everything bigger in Texas' including capex upside
data.description:
CenterPoint Energy (NYSE:CNP) wobbles between small gains and losses in Wednesday's trading as Jefferies initiates coverage with a Buy rating and $42 price target, saying confidence in its 8.3% earnings per share growth forecast vs. 7.2% market consensus is supported by the company's track record of meeting or exceeding its earnings guidance in recent years, combined with the Houston area's strong growth prospects.

Jefferies analyst Julien Dumoulin-Smith sees Houston's load growth as differentiated from other regions due to data centers playing a smaller role in the already otherwise strong growth outlook, which includes 40%-60% from energy refining and exports.

CenterPoint (NYSE:CNP) is poised for a strong recovery post-Hurricane Beryl, and regulatory headwinds are easing with its system resiliency plan advancing and mobile gen asset transferring, Dumoulin-Smith says, and four successful rate case settlements so far provide strong confidence while $47.5B in 10-year capex points to robust long-term growth even before the 10-year plan roll-forward mid-2025 and his forecast for $6B capital spending upside.

data.title: Sanofi licensing deal boosts Nurix shares
data.description:
• Shares of Nurix Therapeutics (NASDAQ:NRIX) traded higher on Wednesday after the company announced a licensing agreement with Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) to advance a clinical program targeted at autoimmune diseases.
  • The exclusive licensing deal covers a previously undisclosed Nurix (NASDAQ:NRIX) program designed to target a molecule involved in certain autoimmune diseases, the San Francisco, California-based developer of protein degrader therapies said.
  • Nurix (NASDAQ:NRIX) added that the licensed target is separate from its previously disclosed STAT6 degrader program, for which Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) decided to collaborate in 2024, expanding their original partnership in 2019.
  • The latest program allows the company to receive an extra $465M in development, regulatory, and commercial milestones. Additionally, NRIX will be eligible to receive royalties and an option to jointly develop and market the program in the U.S. in exchange for 50% of profits or losses.

data.title: Trump weighs announcing creation of external revenue service - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump is weighing announcing the establishment of the so-called external revenue service (ERS) that would collect all dues received from foreign sources, CBS reported, citing sources familiar with the matter.

Trump has made the promise of levying more tariffs on imported goods from China and other countries a cornerstone of his election campaign in 2024. His first term from 2017 to 2021 was marked by similar tariff measures that eventually sparked a trade war.

The potential ERS announcement comes even as the federal government already has a system in place for collecting tariffs in imported goods through U.S. Customs and Border Protection. 

It's still unclear whether the new ERS would fully assume the agency's duties, and its creation might contradict Trump's pledge to reduce federal bureaucracy, CBS reported, noting that Trump's final decision was unclear as of Wednesday afternoon

Investors are awaiting a 4:00 p.m. ET Rose Garden event where Trump is slated to unveil his much-awaited tariffs strategy. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Stocks tread water ahead of Trump's tariff plan; Musk said to be leaving govt role
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages stepped back from intraday highs Wednesday afternoon, ahead of President Donald Trump outlining a plan to impose reciprocal tariffs on imports coming into the U.S.   

The S&P 500 (SP500) was flat, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.1%, relinquishing stronger advances. Each average had fallen more than 1% early in the session. The Dow (DJI) straddled the flat line. It had lost nearly 1% earlier.

In the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 1 basis point to 4.21%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) gained 5 basis points to 3.92% as prices fell. 

Investors were waiting for details on the Trump administration's “Liberation Day” plan to impose large-scale tariffs on trading partners. The administration so far has said tariffs will "start with all countries" and will be "effective immediately."

President Trump was slated to appear in the Rose Garden at 4 pm ET. Sky News reported Wednesday the administration was aiming to band levies by country and industry. The Wall Street Journal reported Tuesday that the U.S. Trade Representative's office was preparing a third option for tariffs that would be lower than a potential 20% universal levy.

Shares of Trump Media & Technology Group (DJT) fell -6.6% on Wednesday. Meanwhile, Tesla (TSLA) shares +5.4%, reversing course after a Politico report said Trump had told his inner circle that Elon Musk would be stepping down from his government role and returning to his businesses, citing three Trump insiders. The White House's press secretary in a post on X called the report "garbage." 

Musk has become an increasingly polarizing figure since fronting Trump's initiative to slash the federal government's size, and ire against him appears to have dented sales of his Tesla (TSLA) electric vehicles. 

Tesla's (TSLA) share-price gain helped drive the Consumer Discretionary sector (XLY) up +1.9%. Overall, eight of the 11 S&P 500 (SP500) sectors rose. 

Ahead of the tariff announcement, the ADP National Employment Report released showed March U.S. private sector employment came in at +155K vs. +120K consensus and +84K prior, upwardly revised from +77K. 

Elsewhere in markets, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) -0.4% at 103.81. "The harsher the [tariffs] announcement is perceived to be, the greater the likelihood that the dollar will weaken, as traders factor in a scenario of slower US growth, potentially forcing the Federal Reserve to cut rates faster than previously expected," Ricardo Evangelista, senior analyst at ActivTrades, said. 

For the Federal Reserve, a strategy of “looking through” tariffs is weaker now than during Trump 1.0 because inflation is running above target, Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at GlobalData TS Lombard, said. 

The Fed “won’t cut interest rates until there are unambiguous signs of weakness in the economy (particularly in the labor market). And that is bad news for financial markets, particularly compared to the situation in 2024 when the Fed was cutting rates pre-emptively,” Perkins said in a note.

“A Fed that is waiting for something to go wrong in the economy is by definition more reactive, and in danger of falling behind the curve," Perkins said. 

Besides new tariffs, 25% levies on cars not made in the U.S. and secondary tariffs on nations that purchase crude oil from Venezuela were set to go into effect this week, the Trump administration has said. 

Wall Street's major averages finished mixed Tuesday in the jumpstart of Q2 action.

data.title: M-tron awarded over $12M production contract from major supplier to Boeing, Airbus
data.description:
• M-tron (NYSE:MPTI) on Wednesday announced a multi-year agreement valued at more than $12 million from a top supplier to the avionics and aerospace industries including Boeing and Airbus.
  • The contract includes over 45 different products, including high-performance radio frequency filters, crystal resonators, TCXOs, OCXOs, and precision clock oscillators.
  • MPTI is +2.06% to $47.37.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Carnival Corporation poised to snap six sessions of losses
data.description:
Carnival Corporation (NYSE:CCL) shares were on track to snap six straight sessions of losses on Wednesday, as it gained 2.17% to $19.81 during afternoon trading.

The cruise operator lost nearly 9% in the preceding six session. The stock closed in the red at $19.39 on Tuesday.

In the year so far, the company has declined nearly 22%, compared to a 4% loss in the broader S&P500 index. However, in the past year, Carnival Corporation’s shares have gained over 23%.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating, the company has a Strong Buy rating with a score of 4.65 out of 5 lifted by an A in profitability and a B- rating in growth and momentum.

Seeking Alpha analysts had a Buy recommendation for the firm’s shares and the Wall Street community echoed the sentiment with a similar rating. Overall, 22 analysts gave the company a Buy and above rating while 6 recommended to Hold the stock while 2 rated it as a Strong Sell. 

Last month the company delivered its first quarter earnings where it posted a non-GAAP EPS of $0.13 beating estimates by $0.11 and a revenue of $5.81B surpassing analysts’ projections by $70M.

However, its shares were dragged down by its FQ2 EPS guidance which it pegged at $0.22, double from a year ago, but a penny below expectations.

Seeking Alpha analyst Mehul Singh believes Carnival Corporation stands to further progress in its post-COVID rebound benefitting from the bankruptcy of smaller players. Further, Carnival’s competitive pricing and the cruise industry’s high barriers to entry, positions it well for growth, he argued.

data.title: Marsh McLennan Agency acquires Arthur Hall Insurance
data.description:
• Marsh McLennan Agency, a business of Marsh (NYSE:MMC) announced the acquisition of Arthur Hall Insurance, a West Chester, Pennsylvania-based full-service insurance agency.
  • Terms of the acquisition were not disclosed.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: DraftKings shares rise after six consecutive sessions of losses
data.description:
DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG) shares snap six straight sessions of losses, as the stock was up 3.1% at $34.22 on Wednesday.

The digital sports entertainment and gaming company lost nearly 15% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has lost nearly 10% so far this year, while in the year 2024, DKNG climbed nearly 11%, compared to a 24% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

DKNG is down 17% over the past one month.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, DKNG has a Buy rating with a score of 3.6 out of 5. The company received D in the prospect of profitability, C in momentum, while it got a A growth factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, out of 33 analysts, 21 gave DKNG a strong Buy rating. Seven analysts have given the stock a Buy recommendation, while five recommended Hold. None see the stock as a Sell. 

Seeking Alpha analysts are positive and see the stock as a Buy.

"The excellent and accelerating growth momentum will likely continue in 2025 as DKNG's customer base swells," pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

data.title: Non-store and on-line shopping to fuel $5.42 trillion in 2025 retail sales - NRF
data.description:
The National Retail Foundation expects retail sales to reach $5.42 trillion this year, an increase of 2.7% to 3.7% from last year, thanks to continued momentum in the economy and led by strength in non-store and online shopping.

“While we do expect slower growth, consumer fundamentals remain intact, supported by low unemployment, slower but steady income growth, and solid household finances. Consumer spending is not unraveling,” said NRF Chief Economist Jack Kleinhenz.

While the group cautioned that uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade policy is weighing on consumer and business confidence, the NRF does not believe this will result in an immediate drop in consumer spending.

With the implementation of tariffs, the NRF expects the personal consumption expenditure price index (“PCE”) to remain unchanged at the 2.5% level. Additionally, household balance sheets remain healthy, credit card and loan delinquencies have risen but remain in line with the pre-pandemic trend, and the consumer credit outlook should remain healthy as long as the labor market remains solid.

Related tickers: Consumer Staples Select SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLP), Consumer Discretionary Select SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLY).
